speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five of survivors of the Shoah interview with Ilka Zygmuntowicz, March 3, 1996. So you were telling us about going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They put us in hermetically sealed cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't count them, but it was packed like sardines, you know. And I remember a terrible smell because there was-- you know, from the people got sick. So all, all the unpleasant things of the body was all in the car. And people were crying. Of course, children were crying. It was a horrible journey. In fact, when we arrived on November 22, 1942, a few people in our car were dead already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were people talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What were people talking about? Some were crying. Some were praying. It was not a normal conversation. And somebody said [NON-ENGLISH]. Where are they taking us? When are we going to get off?

speaker: interviewee
text: They promised us food and things. When are we going to get there? They couldn't wait to get there. We didn't know. And then we were traveling without knowing where we are going at all. What your parents telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember too much conversation. I don't remember. I think everybody was very much into themselves and grieving. But I do remember-- not so much telling-- but my mother was trying to-- like she usually used to do-- to put her arms around and embrace us all. It's like the saying Yiddish, [YIDDISH], like cuddle up all to the parents and just hoping to stay together. We didn't know. We didn't have the slightest inclination where we are going. And then we came, and the time stopped. We were scared. And everything was so fast. [GERMAN] quickly, you know, the Nazis. And then I saw some people in, in striped--

speaker: interviewer
text: We just stopped the tape for a sound problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived at the train station, the doors swung open. And the Nazis were chasing. Fast! Out, out, out. Leave all the packages. And we didn't understand why or anything. There were no questions asked. You just obeyed or you died. We had to be [NON-ENGLISH]. That's why I always relate to the story that my mother read to me. And then I saw a sign, and it said, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' Work makes free. Ironically, when I was a child my father used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. You have to, have to know three things-- Torah, occupation, and how to swim.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 'arbeit macht frei'-- it's nothing wrong. Except we didn't know at that time yet, that the only way we can be free is death. Was our liberator in Auschwitz. There was no freedom. But everything was disguised. Everything-- in fact, I wrote a poem about Mengele at the, at the gate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the time, we didn't know, because they-- the normal language was not normal there. Everything was the opposite. For instance, when they would want to send somebody to the gas chambers, they said [NON-ENGLISH]. They would use language-- normally, to the right. You know, you make a right turn. You make a left turn. A right-hand turn. There to the right, to the left, meant selections, life or death, who would go to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't know. We were not prepared. That's what they said. They say that-- there is a saying in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. It's not so. What you don't know can hurt you a lot. Maybe this is one of the reasons why I am telling people to know, to recognize. So when we came there at the gate, we saw the gate, and they lined up-- they separated men from women. And at the gate was standing some doctor. Didn't know at the time their names, but later I found out that one was Mengele. How did he look to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he look? What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember him. He was wearing an SS uniform, swastika, and looked calm and collect. Was wearing white gloves, which reminds me that my family used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. That a murderer with white gloves is the worst one because it's disguised. Gentleman wear white gloves. And when somebody wears them, you take them for a gentleman. So he was standing there, and he was just motioning to the left and to the right, and making selections. We didn't know what it meant. And at that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get to see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your father, I mean, when he was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just saw when they took him away, but that's all. And then they took away my little brother, Srulek, my little sister , Ziesel. My mother and I were sent to the side to go to the camp. And when my mother saw that they took away my brother and sister, she turned to me and she said, Itka, [SOBBING] you're a big girl. I have to go with the little children. I can't leave them alone. But remember, my child, no matter what will happen to you, don't become bitter and hateful. Don't let them destroy you. It was the last time I saw my family. My mother could have been alive, but she didn't want to abandon the children. Her [NON-ENGLISH] was tested. But I didn't know at the time what the right and the left-- because it was disguised-- a fraudulent-- [INAUDIBLE] which had a Red Cross sign on it. And all my life I knew Red Cross stands for help. And we were promised to get help and better medication, so I was so glad. And also, they gave us the impression-- who is old, who is sick, who is pregnant, it's hard to work-- they're going to get all right. And I said, look, it doesn't even sound like the same Nazis. And I was grateful at the time. And then, the worst part was when I found out-- since we didn't know-- I never said goodbye to any of them. And one thing, everything that I loved and held sacred, everything was gone. My family-- that was the last day on earth for my family. Thousands of Jews from, from the ghetto of Nowe Miasto came together in the beginning of a living hell for me. But we didn't know it yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They marched us in. And from the start, I lost freedom. And we were taken to a place where there's supposed to be-- told us we were going to take a bath. And we came in. I was clutching a little photograph in my-- everything was thrown away but a little photograph from my family. The told me to give it to them. I didn't want to, so I got beaten. And I realized later that I might get killed, so I gave away the last shred of my past. They put on a tattoo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mine was two, five, six, seven, three, which means that 25,673 people before me had those numbers. However, those that they sent to the gas chamber, they didn't put on numbers. So what seemed a misfortune at that time-- found out I have a number. I'm alive. The others didn't have numbers, but neither do they have life. And later, they shaved our hair, heads, and all hair in any private places. For the first time in my life-- I came from a home where I was very modest, you know. I wasn't wearing slacks at home. I never undressed, even from my own parents in front, since when I was already little older, you know. And here you had to disrobe completely naked before strangers. Some girls had their monthly cycle and nothing to shield themselves with. It was horrible. And then they shaved us all. All of a sudden, our past was gone, everything. And we went into the shower. We went to one place and went out to another. There was a whole mountain of striped clothes, which was the lager. 'Lager' means camp-- concentration camp, except that was not a concentration. It was a killing center where-- death factory, because I came to Auschwitz-Birkenau. But there was Auschwitz, and there was Auschwitz-Birkenau. And Birkenau had crematoriums and, and gas chambers. And we were given those clothes, and we put on-- the same number that we had on the arm was sewn on here. From then on, I was no longer Itka. I became number two, five, six, seven, three. Except-- each time they called my number, I was so angry. And under my breath, I would say-- under my breath, I used to say 'Itka.' Maybe to you, I'm a number, but I'm not. I will never-- I cannot [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm Itka. I remember distinctly. Each time when they said it, I said, no, I will never see myself through your eyes. And with each guard, they had in another pile, wooden clogs. Some were fortunate that they got a matching pair. I was not so fortunate. I had an unmatched pair. And we got an enamel bowl. We were told if we lose the bowl or if we're going to use it for-- to relieve ourselves, we're not going to get another bowl. If they catch us relieving in it, we're going to get hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, what are they talking about? Who would want to relieve themself in a bowl that you're eating? Because I didn't know the conditions of the concentration camps. And then we walked out. And we looked at each other, and we all looked the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: From that, they brought us to our barrack. The barrack had bunk beds. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of them had one blanket. Some didn't even have a blanket. There were three, three bunk beds like [INAUDIBLE]. I remember they were very low, because when you were sitting on it, you couldn't sit up straight. At night we slept like sardines, you know. And we came in there, and all of a sudden-- no more. We became [NON-ENGLISH]. We had to be silent no matter what they did, or die. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still naive, and I kept on asking the girls who were there, when will we see the parents, the families? And there was silence. I couldn't understand. Is it because they didn't want to break our hearts, or was it ordered? This I will not know till this-- till this, this day. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there I was-- I was 13 when the war broke out, and this was in 1939. And this was in-- 16, I think, something like that. And nobody would say anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In concentration camp, the reason what I found out later for the-- so I kept on asking. I wouldn't give up. Finally one said, you might as well tell her, because that's where we're all going to go. And she pointed to the smoke, which I don't, didn't know what it was. I thought maybe somebody has a factory there or something-- the smoke that was coming from the gas chambers, from the crematoriums. And she said, this is where our loved ones are. That's where we're all going to go. And at that point, if I could have died, it would have been welcome. But you know, I remember when I was a little girl, my father and mother took me to a funeral [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Jewish cemetery. And when we were there-- and I told you that I always noticed everything and wanted to know why. So I noticed that at the gate were some graves. And then we walked quite a distance, and there were a lot of graves. And those were separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked my father, [NON-ENGLISH], Daddy, why is this grave separate? And he said, this is people who committed suicide. According to the Jewish law, this is an offense to God and to the parents. And he mentioned-- he said it's like spitting God and your parents in the house. So it's not allowed, no matter what, to, to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the despair, I remembered this. And I said, I cannot commit suicide because that's, that's spitting my parents. They were so good to me. They gave the life. But I prayed, dear God, take me, too. [SOBBING] That was-- the pain was just tearing my heart. As I was sitting in the worst circumstances and crying, and all of a sudden I could give up. I didn't care at that time about anything. In Auschwitz, when they made the appell, the counting, if you were unaccountable or if you didn't stand or if you looked sick or sit down, you would go to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting. I didn't want to go out. But I was sitting. They gave bread, and I was hungry. But I couldn't eat. How can you eat when you feel something? And I saved that piece of bread. I couldn't eat it. And in the morning, I woke up and it was gone. That was the final push that I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a girl-- I don't remember her last name-- she came from a prison, from [NON-ENGLISH]. So she must have done something before the war yet, or maybe during the war. I don't know. I didn't know her. Said to me, poor Itka. Somebody stole her bread. And I was so grateful for the little compassion that she showed. The next day I didn't eat either. I just couldn't. I was just-- I woke up in the middle of the night. To my horror, Esther is eating my bread. And I was just shattered to pieces. I couldn't understand. She was the one who said poor Itka that somebody stole the bread. And she was the one who stole it. This was the last straw. And at that point I said I don't care what's happening now. I didn't intend at any point in my life to commit suicide, but I just was ready to give up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was sitting crying and everything, a girl came over. And she asked me, why are you crying? I didn't answer. But there was something urgent in her voice, something caring, it sounded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't know why I was crying, so she thought maybe I'm hungry. So she took a piece of bread from hers, and she broke off. She said, take it. I was afraid to accept the bread, [SOBBING] because knowing what's happening to us, that we were brought here to die either in the gas chambers or of sickness or of fatigue or whatever. I was afraid to have a friend. I couldn't afford to lose anyone, anyone. But it was something in her voice and in her face, and maybe the need in me, because I always loved people. I always was with people, and I cared. I always had friends. And I knew if I accepted bread, she was offering more. So I finally took the piece of bread. And Bina and I became inseparable. Bina [NON-ENGLISH]. She became like more than a sister, because under those conditions, you might not think a lot of a piece of bread. In Auschwitz, a piece of bread means prolonging life, and maybe another day could mean liberation or just another day of survival. And there-- even sometimes, parents-- you know, nobody could help, too much, each other, because there was so little-- so just a few that we have some control of our life. Bina and I used to do something very funny and very tragic. We used to exchange. Sometimes I would give her a little soup and she would give a piece of bread just to know that we had some control. Plus, we would always-- regardless, we trusted each other. I knew without having somebody to love, to care, to look out for, that I wouldn't have any meaning. I knew I had no family anymore. So we would keep together with our relations. We could not hold this [INAUDIBLE] separate. And in concentration camp was a law. The worst offence any girl could do there was to take from another girl, because that was like taking life. That was the only time somebody stole from me-- the bread. And Bina and I trusted each other. We kept our bread. Some days when she had the worse day, I would give her a piece of mine, sometimes. But we did-- accepting of the friendship and the bread-- we both made a pledge to each other, to ourselves. We're going to live and die with menschlichkeit. Everybody dies at one point or another. So it's not just the quantity of life-- the quality. Because we knew this is the only spiritual assistance that we could do. And we started do everything to sabotage. We worked on the job. Unfortunately, at the beginning when we worked, the [INAUDIBLE] commander, which was-- used to call, like work outside-- useless work carrying stone or brick or gravel from one place to the other. We couldn't-- there wasn't a lot to sabotage, but later on we did when there were other things that we could. And Bina and I became inseparable.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to describe a day in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We would get up in the morning. To begin with, we slept and worked, and wore the same clothes all the time. Every few months they would disinfect, because unfortunately, a lot of lice would set. So we really were worse than the Jews in Egypt. We had all the plagues.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] A big pleasure was if you could undress the dress in time to clean off a little of the-- it's unfortunate it's very graphic, but I'm telling it as it is. And we would-- still at dark. I never had a clock so I don't know what time it was. But it was always dark. Some people say it was 4:30, 5:30. But I couldn't tell you the time. I didn't have a watch. All I possessed is what I wore-- the shift, and the, the unmatched clogs, and the bowl.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know, but it was dark, and we had to be counted. [NON-ENGLISH], it was called, the counting. Everybody has to be a accountable, alive and dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we used, [NON-ENGLISH], one Jew for the other is responsible. They used it there, but I don't ever think-- you know, Hitler's law was a reversal of all the Mosaic laws. It says, thou shall not murder. They murdered. Thou shall not steal. Shall love your God-- freedom. Exactly the opposite, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every day we had to stand accounted there. And they made it responsible. So if, let's say, one would want to run away. And if you had the heart to say how can I endanger all, because then we would all suffer.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could sometimes stay for  hours and for days. Dead or alive, everybody had to be accountable. So we stayed at appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got something which resembled-- supposed to be coffee, but resembled muddy water. And we wished we could have had more of it. Piece of bread, sometimes a little piece of-- what is it called? It was like a cheese, like a-- it's like Limburger cheese here.

speaker: interviewee
text: It smelled a little, but to us, everything tasted good because we were starved all the time. All you could think about was food. And we would get [INAUDIBLE]. There was no water-- drinking water. There was water, but we were told that this water is not good. And after the appell, we would always march in five. [SPEAKING IN GERMAN] Still I hear it in my-- and on both sides, the, the guards, the dogs, and marching. When you marched out, sometimes we never knew who would come back. Because sometimes, at random, there were selections at the, at the gate. They would take away. I remember there were instances where if one of us looked pale, the other would sometimes-- I would do it to Bina. I would say, [NON-ENGLISH]. You look so pale. And she would pinch my cheek to make it look red. Because first of all, they did everything to make us sick. Then they would-- they didn't need that excuse. Then they would take it out because she's sick. Just-- it was [NON-ENGLISH]. Another thing which was the horrible thing, there was no-- you couldn't go to relieve yourself. Then I understood why the bowls, because at night there was one big pail, bucket, for all. But sometimes it was over-filling and everything else. And other times, we had to go to the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like a piece of cement covered with wood, cut holes. And you would have to wait your turn to go in. You could not have a certain time. And because of the conditions and everything, a lot of the girls got diarrhea. Once you got the diarrhea, you were finished, because if you smelled, if you couldn't keep clean, if you didn't look well, that immediately to the, the gas chambers. Usually when we walked, we tried to stay on the inside to be less visible. When it was cold-- in the coldest-- Bina and I would try to stay next to each other. So we ran back to warm to the other. And then we went out to work in the worst condition. The worst thing was you couldn't help each other. You were not allowed in their presence to help each other. Even our language was imprisoned for two reasons. Once-- we couldn't speak at roll time. The second, from the barrack-- probably 1,000 girls must have been in one barrack from different countries. It was like the Tower of Babel. Everybody spoke a different language. I was grateful that I could speak with Bina because she could speak Yiddish and Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bina was born in Warsaw. At that time, I didn't even ask her too much about the family, if she had brothers and sisters. I don't know why. It was-- there was more urgent issues. Are you going to survive today? Are you going to get a piece of bread? Are you going to stay-- you know, all those survival issues. You didn't think-- have much time to think.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know that she was born in Warsaw. She was born-- she was three years older than I. She was born October 22, 1923. And we became closer than sisters, because those conditions make.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at work, carried the gravel and the stone from one place to the other. Sometimes they would mock us. And I remember they would point to the gas chamber. Where is your God now? And inside myself I said, our God is here, but where is yours? Where is yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, I'm going to say something. I don't know if many survivors said this here. Till I came to, to the Holocaust, I did and believed and followed the instructions my parents and my grandmother taught me, our religion taught me, because I didn't know any other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was in Auschwitz where my faith in God was born. Isn't that strange? Because when I saw what the Nazis became when they transgressed God's commandments-- and they did murder. They did steal. They did everything the opposite of our commandments.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I pledged to myself to live and to die with dignity, with menschlichkeit. I understood the word menschlichkeit. I understood everything, the sacrifice. And we did a lot to help each other, the girls. The girl, Esther, was the only exception that I experienced in Auschwitz that was-- you know, that stole from somebody. And I realized one thing-- that only in extremity-- extremities-- you know, the conditions like this where everybody was equal. Nobody had anything. The millionaire and the pauper, we all were starved. We were all slaves. Worse than slaves, because a slave has some rights-- gets fed, gets things. We were worse than them. They brought us to destroy us. Even the ones who send there-- were sent, who would take a little longer. And I realized all those conditions, and then the work, you know. And that's when I realized, and I said to myself-- I started to examine the values of my parents. I realized the importance of their love.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine being so hated that people wanted to destroy you? To tell you that you are subhuman, that you are a vermin. All those things and all the conditions that was going on. Is this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and then, when we went to work, and when we came back-- constant selection, constant with people dying. And mountains, mountains, of body. Around us was the electric, electrical barbed wires. Above us, smoke. Beneath us, muddy, muddy ground, and constantly people dying in [INAUDIBLE] columns.
